questionText: For real numbers $x$ and $y$, determine the logical relationship between the statement $P:\ x^2 + y^2 = 0$ and the statement $Q:\ x = 0$ and $y = 0$. State which of $\Rightarrow$, $\Leftarrow$ or $\Leftrightarrow$ connects them, and prove your claim.

steps.description: Write down the two statements and the domain.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ x^2 + y^2 = 0, \qquad Q:\ x = 0 \text{ and } y = 0 \qquad (x, y \in \mathbb{R})
steps.explanation: We record both claims and, crucially, the fact that $x$ and $y$ are real. The domain will turn out to be exactly what makes the equivalence work.

steps.description: Note that a connective claim must be tested in both directions.
steps.explanation: To decide between the one-way arrows and the two-way arrow we check each direction independently. Only if both hold may we write $\Leftrightarrow$.

steps.description: Prove the easy direction Q ⇒ P.
steps.explanation: If both variables are zero, substituting straight in gives a sum of zero. This direction is immediate.
So the harder question is whether the equation can force both variables to vanish. We now attack $P\Rightarrow Q$.

steps.description: State the key fact about real squares.
steps.workingLatex: x^2 \ge 0 \ \text{ and } \ y^2 \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } x, y \in \mathbb{R}
steps.explanation: Squaring any real number can never produce a negative result, so both terms sit at or above zero. This non-negativity is the engine of the whole proof.

steps.description: Explain why the sum cannot hide cancellation.
steps.explanation: If neither term is negative, one cannot cancel the other; the only way their total reaches zero is for both to be zero. A single positive term would push the sum strictly above zero.

steps.workingLatex: x^2 + y^2 = 0, \ x^2 \ge 0, \ y^2 \ge 0 \ \Rightarrow\ x^2 = 0 \ \text{ and } \ y^2 = 0
steps.explanation: Combining the equation with the two inequalities pins each square to zero. There is no other option consistent with all three facts.

steps.description: Deduce the values of x and y.
steps.explanation: The only real number whose square is zero is zero itself, so each variable must vanish. This gives exactly the statement $Q$.
The equation really does force both variables to be zero. So the forward implication is established.

steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{ and } \ Q \Rightarrow P \ \Rightarrow\ P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: Each statement implies the other, so they are logically equivalent and the correct symbol is $\Leftrightarrow$. The two conditions describe the very same situation.

steps.description: Highlight the reliance on the real domain.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Over } \mathbb{C}: \ x = 1, \ y = i \ \Rightarrow\ x^2 + y^2 = 1 + (-1) = 0, \ \text{ yet } Q \text{ is false}
steps.explanation: If complex values were allowed, a square could be negative and the equivalence would collapse. The non-negativity of squares, and hence the whole result, depends on staying within the real numbers.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $P \Leftrightarrow Q$: for real $x$ and $y$, $x^2 + y^2 = 0$ if and only if $x = 0$ and $y = 0$, because a sum of non-negative squares is zero only when each square is zero.
